confirmed.title: hardDelete: count-then-delete without transaction allows deletion of client with active project links
confirmed.description: The `hardDelete` method reads the project-association count (line 265) then, in a separate operation, deletes the client (line 274). Between the count check and the delete, a concurrent `assignClientToProject` call can insert a `ProjectClient` row, meaning the client ends up deleted while a live project association still references it. The DB may cascade-delete the `ProjectClient` row (depending on FK rules), silently orphaning project records or causing inconsistent state depending on the cascade configuration.
confirmed.root_cause: Guard-then-delete pattern without wrapping both operations in a `$transaction`, leaving a TOCTOU window.
confirmed.impact: A client linked to a project can be hard-deleted if another request creates the association concurrently, potentially violating referential integrity or silently removing a project link. Confirmed: ProjectClient FK has onDelete: Cascade on client — concurrent insert+delete will result in the ProjectClient row silently disappearing when the client is deleted, destroying the association without any guard.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap both the count check and the delete inside a `$transaction`: `await this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { const count = await tx.projectClient.count({...}); if (count > 0) throw new ConflictException(...); await tx.client.delete({...}); });`
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent `assignClientToProject` and `hardDelete` calls never result in a client being deleted when it has active project associations
The entire guard-then-delete sequence executes within a single DB transaction
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: (1) code_evidence is verbatim at lines 265-274. (2) No $transaction wraps hardDelete — grep confirms only one $transaction usage in the file (findAll). (3) Schema shows ProjectClient.client has onDelete: Cascade, meaning the race window does silently cascade-delete the association. Finding stands.
confirmed.notes: The same pattern exists in update() (findUnique then update) but the consequence there is lower severity since update does not violate referential integrity.

confirmed.title: documents.update: prisma.document.update has no deletedAt filter — can mutate soft-deleted document in race window
confirmed.description: `findOne(id)` (line 173) checks `document.deletedAt` and throws NotFoundException if the document is soft-deleted. However, `prisma.document.update({ where: { id } })` (line 174) uses only the `id` filter with no `deletedAt: null` guard. If a concurrent soft-delete sets `deletedAt` between the `findOne` call and the `update` call, the update will silently succeed on a logically-deleted document, resurrecting its state partially or overwriting the `deletedAt`-based tombstone.
confirmed.root_cause: The existence/soft-delete check and the write are two separate DB roundtrips with no transaction or atomic WHERE clause combining both conditions.
confirmed.impact: In a concurrent scenario, a soft-deleted document can be updated (e.g., its metadata changed), violating the soft-delete invariant that deleted documents are immutable. If `updateDocumentDto` includes `deletedAt: null`, it would hard-undelete the document without any audit trail.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Change the update where clause to `{ id, deletedAt: null }` and handle the Prisma P2025 'record not found' error as a NotFoundException, or wrap findOne + update in a `$transaction`.
acceptance_criteria: Calling update() on a soft-deleted document always returns 404
No update succeeds on a document with deletedAt set
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: code_evidence is verbatim at lines 172-181. No $transaction and no deletedAt filter in the update where clause. Finding stands.
confirmed.notes: The same TOCTOU is present for the remove() → findOne() → update({deletedAt: new Date()}) pattern, though its impact is lower: double-soft-delete is idempotent.

confirmed.title: CreateDocumentDto.size has no @IsNumber() or @Min(0) — negative/float sizes accepted
confirmed.description: The `size` field carries only `@IsNotEmpty()` — which checks for non-empty/null/undefined but does NOT validate that the value is a number, an integer, or non-negative. `@IsNotEmpty()` on a number type passes `0` and any negative value. The service's upper-bound check (`size > MAX_DOCUMENT_SIZE_BYTES` in documents.service.ts:50) only rejects oversized values; it does not reject negative sizes (e.g., `size: -1` would satisfy `!isNotEmpty && -1 < 200_000_000`). There is no `@IsInt()`, `@IsNumber()`, or `@Min(0)` decorator. A client can store a document with `size: -1` or `size: 3.14`, which is semantically invalid for a byte count and may cause incorrect quota calculations.
confirmed.root_cause: Missing class-validator numeric type decorators on the `size` field; only an upper-bound guard was added at the service layer.
confirmed.impact: Negative or fractional byte counts can be persisted, leading to incorrect summaries (e.g., `budgetHoursTotal` analogues for storage), and bypassing any quota logic that trusts the stored size.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Add `@IsInt()` and `@Min(0)` (both from `class-validator`) to `size` in `CreateDocumentDto`. Also check that `UpdateDocumentDto` (via `PartialType`) inherits these constraints.
acceptance_criteria: POST /documents with size: -1 returns 400 Bad Request
POST /documents with size: 3.14 returns 400 Bad Request
POST /documents with size: 0 succeeds (zero-byte documents are valid)
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: full DTO read confirms only @IsNotEmpty() on the size field. Imports are IsString, IsNotEmpty, IsOptional, IsUUID, IsUrl, IsIn, MaxLength, MinLength — no IsInt, IsNumber, or Min imported. Service-level guard at line 50 only checks size > MAX (200MB), does not reject negatives. Finding stands.
confirmed.notes: The service-side check `createDocumentDto.size > MAX_DOCUMENT_SIZE_BYTES` is bypassed by any negative value, and only applies in create(), not in update().

confirmed.title: documents.update accepts projectId change without checking user access to the new project
confirmed.description: `UpdateDocumentDto` extends `PartialType(CreateDocumentDto)` (see update-document.dto.ts), which means `projectId` is an optional field in the update payload. The `update()` method passes `updateDocumentDto` directly to `prisma.document.update()` without checking whether the caller has access to the new `projectId`. The `OwnershipGuard` at the controller layer only verifies ownership of the document being updated — it does NOT verify that the destination project is accessible to the caller. A document owner can move their document to any arbitrary project (including confidential or restricted projects) by including a `projectId` in the PATCH payload.
confirmed.root_cause: The service `create()` correctly calls `assertCanAccessProject(createDocumentDto.projectId, ...)` but `update()` has no equivalent check for a projectId change.
confirmed.impact: A document owner can attach their document to any project, including ones they have no visibility into, potentially leaking document content to project members of the target project who can now list/read the document.
confirmed.suggested_fix: In `update()`, detect if `updateDocumentDto.projectId` is defined and differs from the current document's `projectId`; if so, call `accessScope.assertCanAccessProject(updateDocumentDto.projectId, currentUser, ...)`. The `update()` signature needs to accept `currentUser`.
acceptance_criteria: PATCH /documents/:id with a projectId the caller has no access to returns 403
PATCH /documents/:id with the caller's own projectId succeeds
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: (1) UpdateDocumentDto is confirmed as `PartialType(CreateDocumentDto)` — projectId IS passable. (2) Controller PATCH calls `documentsService.update(id, updateDocumentDto)` with no currentUser. (3) OwnershipGuard only checks document ownership via OwnershipService.isOwner() — no body inspection for projectId. (4) No assertCanAccessProject call in update(). Finding stands.
confirmed.notes: The create() method correctly enforces this check at line 57-62, but update() has no equivalent.

confirmed.title: ThirdPartiesService.update: LEGAL_ENTITY invariant checked without transaction — concurrent partial updates can violate it
confirmed.description: The LEGAL_ENTITY invariant (no contactFirstName/contactLastName allowed) is validated by merging the incoming dto fields with the current DB row (`existing`). However, this `findUnique` + invariant-check + `update` sequence is NOT wrapped in a transaction. Two concurrent PATCH requests — one changing `type` to LEGAL_ENTITY and another setting `contactFirstName = 'John'` — can both read the same `existing` row, pass their respective invariant checks, then execute their `update` calls. The final DB state may have `type=LEGAL_ENTITY` and `contactFirstName='John'`, violating the invariant.
confirmed.root_cause: Read-check-write pattern for a multi-field business invariant without a serializable transaction or optimistic concurrency (no version/updated_at in WHERE clause).
confirmed.impact: The LEGAL_ENTITY type invariant can be violated via concurrent partial updates, resulting in a DB record in an invalid state that downstream code does not expect.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Wrap `findUnique`, invariant check, and `update` in a `$transaction` with isolation level SERIALIZABLE, or add an `updated_at` version field and use it in the `update WHERE` clause for optimistic concurrency control.
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent PATCH requests that individually pass the invariant check but collectively violate it are rejected
The final state of a third party always satisfies: type=LEGAL_ENTITY implies contactFirstName IS NULL AND contactLastName IS NULL
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: code_evidence is verbatim at lines 115-125. The update at line 140 is a bare prisma.thirdParty.update with no transaction wrapper. The invariant is purely a service-layer check with no DB CHECK constraint backup. Finding stands. Confidence remains medium (concurrent writes are uncommon in single-user admin usage, but the invariant is still breakable under load).
confirmed.notes: This is a true concurrent-write race; under normal single-user traffic it does not manifest. The LEGAL_ENTITY restriction is also only a service-layer invariant — not enforced via a DB CHECK constraint.

confirmed.title: listProjectClients, listTaskAssignees, listProjectMembers return empty list for non-existent parent — should return 404
confirmed.description: `listProjectClients` (clients.service.ts:290), `listTaskAssignees` (third-parties.service.ts:310), and `listProjectMembers` (third-parties.service.ts:323) all skip any existence check on the parent resource. If the `projectId` or `taskId` does not exist, Prisma returns an empty array. The API then returns HTTP 200 with `[]`, which is misleading — a caller cannot distinguish 'the project exists but has no clients' from 'the project does not exist at all'. This violates the REST contract implied by a nested resource route (`/projects/:projectId/clients`).
confirmed.root_cause: No `findUnique` or `count` check on the parent entity before querying the child join table.
confirmed.impact: Callers (including tests and frontend) cannot detect non-existent parent IDs via these endpoints. Integration bugs can be masked by silent 200+[] responses.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Before calling `findMany`, verify the parent exists with `prisma.project.count({ where: { id: projectId } })` and throw NotFoundException if zero. Similarly for task in listTaskAssignees.
confirmed.adversarial_check: Verified: (1) listProjectClients at lines 290-298 verbatim — no parent existence check. (2) listTaskAssignees at lines 310-321 and listProjectMembers at lines 323-334 confirmed — both only do findMany with no parent check. (3) Contrast with getClientProjects which does check client existence first. Finding stands.
confirmed.notes: The same list methods in the same codebase for getClientProjects (line 119) DO correctly check existence first.
